tradeoffs: increasing gm usually increases bias current and power
reducing CL is only valid when the real load permits it

assumptions: Miller compensation creates the dominant pole
the second pole and compensation zero are above UGF
tradeoffs: increasing Cc usually improves stability but reduces GBW and slew rate
increasing gm usually raises current and can reduce phase margin

assumptions: the dominant pole contributes approximately -90 degrees at UGF
one non-dominant pole dominates additional phase lag
no nearby zero materially changes phase
tradeoffs: lowering UGF can improve PM at the cost of speed
raising p2 can improve PM without intentionally sacrificing UGF

assumptions: K is set by the topology-specific resistor/current ratio
tradeoffs: changing K changes both nominal Vref and first-order tempco
higher current can improve settling while increasing power and self-heating

assumptions: matched bipolar devices at current-density ratio N
series resistance and high-level injection are negligible
tradeoffs: larger N increases PTAT voltage but can increase area and mismatch sensitivity

assumptions: curvature and resistor tempco are handled separately
tradeoffs: a correct room-temperature Vref does not guarantee low tempco
residual curvature requires curvature compensation rather than unlimited ratio tuning

limitations: the current topology uses idealized PTAT/CTAT sources rather than PDK bipolar devices
dedicated Vref/tempco/line-regulation parsers are not implemented yet
